hub_subtitle: Conversação para Feiras Internacionais de Turismo & Hotelaria

lesson.menu_title: Listening and Responding -- Native Speakers at Fairs
lesson.menu_desc: Understanding fast native speakers and clarifying in real time -- 25 slides
lesson.subtitle: Aula 7 -- Listening and Responding
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Andrea Aggio | Aula 7 -- Listening and Responding

listenings.text: Hi Andrea, it is David from the Lisbon office. Sorry, I am calling from the fair floor, so there is a lot of background noise. I will keep this quick. We loved your itineraries, and honestly, the gist is that we want to move fast on a group deal. I know my accent can be tricky on the phone, so if you miss anything, just call me back and I will happily rephrase. The main point: send me your best group rate for the low season, and let us set up a short call this week. Talk soon.

Welcome back to our language tips segment. Here is one skill that changes everything at an international fair: active listening. When a native speaker talks fast, do not panic. First, listen for the gist, not every single word. Second, if you lose the thread, it is completely normal to ask the person to slow down. A simple phrase like, would you mind repeating that, sounds professional and confident. Third, paraphrase what you heard to confirm it. When you say, so you are saying, you show that you are engaged. Remember, asking for clarification is a strength, not a weakness.

vocab.title: Match each word to its meaning
defs: to hear and understand what someone says
the way words blend together in fast speech
a person whose first language is English
the way people from a region pronounce words
to understand while someone is speaking
a small word like well that gives you thinking time
to repeat an idea in different words
to say something in a different way
the general meaning of what someone said
to talk more loudly and clearly

parts: At a busy fair, do not panic when a
, ask them to slow down or

gapfill.text: Notice: 'Would you mind' is followed by the -ing form, not 'to': 'Would you mind repeating that?' To describe how someone speaks, use an adverb: speak slowly, speak clearly, speak up.

bank.label: Useful language for clarifying
items: Sorry, I did not catch that.
Could you speak up a little, please?
Do you mean today or this week?
So you are saying the rate is for groups only?
Let me paraphrase to make sure I understood.
Could you rephrase that in simpler words?
I got the gist, thank you.

lf.title: Repeat, check, or confirm?
items: the net rate or the group rate?

lf.text: Notice: 'Would you mind + -ing' politely asks someone to repeat or slow down; 'Do you mean...?' checks which option; 'So you are saying...?' confirms the message in your own words.

items: A supplier speaks very fast. Politely ask them to repeat, using 'Would you mind repeating that?'
You are not sure which price the partner means. Check it with 'Do you mean...?'
Confirm a deal you just heard by paraphrasing: 'So you are saying...?'
The line is noisy and you cannot hear. Ask the person to speak up, then paraphrase to confirm.

answerkey.title: Reveal the answer key
list: Gap-fill: 1 = native speaker, 2 = follow along, 3 = gist, 4 = lose the thread, 5 = rephrase.
Repeat (mind + -ing): 'Would you mind repeating the last part?'
Check meaning: 'Do you mean the group rate or the net rate?'
Confirm: 'So you are saying the offer is only for the low season, right?'
answerkey.note: After 'Would you mind', always use the -ing form: repeating, speaking, slowing down.
